answers.intake_q2: Any vomiting or abdominal pain along with the nausea?
answers.intake_q3: Can you describe the character and exact location of the lower abdominal pain?
answers.intake_a3: dull, cramping, and the location is lower, central abdomine
answers.intake_q4: Any fever, chills, or urinary symptoms like burning or increased frequency?
answers.intake_q5: Any chance you could be pregnant or any possibility of pregnancy?
answers.intake_q6: Any vaginal bleeding or unusual discharge?
answers.intake_a6: I have my period, so vaginal bleeding, but nothing out of the ordinary
answers.intake_q7: Any history of similar symptoms in the past or any known medical conditions related to your abdomen or reproductive system?
answers.intake_q8: Any nausea triggers like food, medications, or recent travel?
answers.intake_q9: Any diarrhea or constipation along with your nausea and abdominal pain?
answers.intake_q10: Any pain or tenderness when you press on your lower abdomen?

note.soap:
S: 
Patient is not pregnant.
The patient is a 51-year-old female presenting with nausea that started approximately one hour ago. She reports associated dull, cramping lower central abdominal pain but denies vomiting, fever, chills, urinary symptoms, diarrhea, constipation, or tenderness on palpation. She is currently menstruating with no unusual vaginal bleeding or discharge. There is no history of similar symptoms or known abdominal or reproductive system conditions. She denies any nausea triggers such as food, medications, or recent travel. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
The working diagnosis is acute nausea with associated lower abdominal cramping likely related to menstrual cycle or a benign gastrointestinal cause given the absence of systemic symptoms or signs of infection. 

Differential: 
1. Menstrual-related nausea and cramping 
2. Gastroenteritis or early viral illness 
3. Functional gastrointestinal disorder (e.g., irritable bowel syndrome) 
4. Early urinary tract infection or pelvic inflammatory process (less likely without urinary symptoms or fever) 

P: 
- First-line medication: 
 Ondansetron 4 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for nausea for up to 48 hours. Avoid if known hypersensitivity to ondansetron. Dosing details were not found in retrieved evidence; prescriber should verify. 

- Alternatives: 
 - Promethazine 12.5–25 mg orally every 4–6 hours as needed for nausea; avoid in patients with respiratory depression or known hypersensitivity. 
 - Metoclopramide 10 mg orally every 6–8 hours as needed; avoid in patients with Parkinson’s disease or seizure disorders. 

- Medication safety considerations: 
 No known medication allergies. Patient is not pregnant. No renal or liver disease reported, so standard dosing applies. 

- Return precautions: 
 Seek urgent care if nausea worsens, vomiting develops, abdominal pain intensifies or becomes localized, fever or chills develop, or if new urinary symptoms occur. 

- Testing: 
 If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

evidence.title: ACOG FAQ: Morning Sickness (Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy)
evidence.snippet: iting of pregnancy? You should talk with your obstetrician-gynecologist (ob-gyn) or other obstetric care provider if nausea and vomiting of pregnancy affects your life and causes you concern. Is there a term for severe nausea and vomiting of pregnancy? Hyperemesis gravidarum is the term for the most severe form of nausea and vomiting of pregnancy. Hyperemesis gravidarum occurs in up to 3 percent of pregnancies. When is hyperemesis gravidarum diagnosed? This condition may be diagnosed when a woman has lost 5 percent of her prepregnancy weight and has other problems related to dehydration , or loss of body fluids (see below). Women with hyperemesis gravidarum need treatment, sometimes in a hospital, to stop the vomiting and restore body fluids. What are the signs of dehydration? Nausea and vomiting can cause you to lose fluids. If fluids are not replaced, it can lead to dehydration. You sh

evidence.title: Cyclical vomiting syndrome - NHS
evidence.snippet:
eks or months until a new episode begins.
Cyclical vomiting syndrome often starts in children around 5 years old and improves as they get older. But it can also affect adults and is often more severe in adults who did not have the condition as a child.
Urgent advice: Get help from NHS 111 now if:
you or your child keep being sick and cannot keep fluid down you or your child are peeing less than usual (or your baby has fewer wet nappies) You can call 111 or get help from 111 online . Call 111 if you're asking about a child under 5 years old.
Immediate action required: Call 999 or go to A&E if you or your child:
vomit blood or have vomit that looks like ground coffee have green vomit (adults) have yellow-green or green vomit (children) have a stiff neck and eye pain when looking at bright lights have a sudden, severe headache have a sudden, severe tummy ache have blue, grey, pale or blotch

If you have a high risk for developing kidney disease , even if you don't have symptoms now. Chronic (long-term) kidney disease (CKD) often doesn't have symptoms in the early stages. Your risk for kidney disease is increased if you:
Have diabetes 
Have high blood pressure 
Have a family health history of kidney disease, diabetes, or high blood pressure 
Have heart disease 
Are over 50 years old 
Smoke 
Have obesity 
If you have kidney problems or possible kidney problems because you have:
Had an abnormal result on a kidney test in the past 
Been di
